subject: LU-6824 ldiskfs: give warning with dir htree growing

values.-1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this
values.+1: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

messages.message: Uploaded patch set 1.

Patch Set 1:

Build Started https://build.hpdd.intel.com/job/lustre-reviews/33234/

Looks good to me.

Patch Set 1: Verified+1

Build Successful 
 
https://build.hpdd.intel.com/job/lustre-reviews/33234/ : SUCCESS

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

(4 comments)

I don't agree about printing a message to the console for this case.  There isn't anything that the user can do about this, even if they saw these messages (which they won't because they are on the MDS console, where users don't usually have access).  They will just run until they hit ENOSPC.

I think a better approach would be to implement the e2fsck support for the EXT4_FEATURE_INCOMPAT_LARGEDIR feature so users don't have this problem at all.

Patch Set 1:

Andreas, Yes agreed this messages deosn't help avoding htree size limitation at all, but this is customer requests. At least, administrator wants to know 
continuously glowing directories and they can let user know "your's directory X is reaching of xx% of maximum htree size" to warn.
If we could have EXT4_FEATURE_INCOMPAT_LARGEDIR quickly, that would be cool. is it also help in existing filesystem without format?
We know this warning messages can't solve problem, but until EXT4_FEATURE_INCOMPAT_LARGEDIR is available, it's a workaround at least administer knows big directory and they can notify users.

Patch Set 1:

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.6/x86_64: (https://testing.hpdd.intel.com/test_sessions/d1381ab6-2690-11e5-92e6-5254006e85c2).  Ran 6 tests.  No failures.

Patch Set 1:

I think for this patch there needs to be some kind of ratelimit on the ext4_warning() messages that are printed to the console.  One option is to put a flag into the in-memory ext4_inode_info EXT4_STATE_* flags that indicates this message was printed for the 50% full case, and it won't print again until it hits 75% or the inode is dropped from memory and the flag is lost.  It would also be better to use d_path() instead of making a new version of that code.  It should be possible to create a vfsmount from the superblock and the s_root dentry something like:

    struct vfsmount vfsmount = { .mnt_root = sb->s_root, .mnt_sb = sb };
    struct path path = { .mnt = &vfsmount, .dentry = dentry };

that is good enough for calling d_path().


I haven't looked at how much effort is needed for adding the INCOMPAT_LARGEDIR feature to e2fsprogs.  I think handling the 3-level htree in e2fsck is probably relatively simple and can be tested with 1KB blocksize directories with only about 100k files (as local ldiskfs mount, not Lustre).  It might be a bit more work to fix the i_size_high usage for directories instead of only regular files, but I think that is mostly a grep for "i_size" or "i_dir_acl" usage and changing the code.  I think it might be reasonable to get a working version in a few days, and then a few days to make e2fsprogs regression tests.  A few more days would be needed to verify that the 3-level htree + > 2GB directory size was working in the ldiskfs code.

There shouldn't be any existing directory inodes with i_dir_acl != 0, since e2fsck already checks for this, so there isn't any danger to existing directories if this suddenly becomes i_size_high if INCOMPAT_LARGEDIR is enabled.

To use INCOMPAT_LARGEDIR would not need any reformatting of the MDT filesystem, only upgrading e2fsprogs and turning the feature on the filesystem via "tune2fs -O largedir /dev/MDT".  There might need to be fixes to the ext4-large-dir.patch in master (and ext4-pdirop.patch in b2_5) if it doesn't handle this new feature correctly.  Since this doesn't change the network protocol it doesn't need any changes to the client or OST, on the MDT ldiskfs.

Patch Set 3: Code-Review-1

(7 comments)

Patch Set 3:

Hi Andreas,

  Ihara suggested that we could pass down FID and print out it  so that
Administrator could got full path itself, what do you think this way?

Regards,
Shilong

Patch Set 3:

The inode already has the FID in the "lma" xattr, so it wouldn't need to be passed down.  The problem is that ldiskfs itself doesn't really "know" about the FID, and the "lma" xattr will not exist for non-Lustre filesystems so this patch couldn't go upstream like that, but it probably would be a much smaller patch than the current one.

Another solution would be to fix the osd-ldiskfs caller to fill out the dentry more fully so that d_path() doesn't oops, but prints out the file and/or directory name before returning.

Another option is for ldiskfs to pass up a different error than -ENOSPC to the callers when the directory is full (e.g. -ENOBUFS "No buffer space available", which would be reasonable in this case also) and then have osd-ldiskfs print a more useful error message including the FID for use by "lfs fid2path".

Of course, the client application should also be getting the ENOSPC errors returned if the directory is full, and it has the full path, so it would be better if the application handled this error properly.

The best solution would be to fix e2fsck to allow 3-level htree, so that the directory wouldn't become full, and there is no need to print an error message.

Patch Set 9: Code-Review-1

(8 comments)

Found a couple more problems with the patch itself that need to be fixed.

Also, since there is now a tunable in "/sys/fs/ldiskfs/{dev}/warning_dir_size" it makes sense to add a test for this, since the limit can be reduced easily and then a few long files created in the directory should trigger it.  This test could be added to sanity.sh test_129 since that is already testing "max_dir_size".

I begin to wonder if 50% full is too low a warning limit, since we don't want to print this warning too often and it is enabled by default.  I think either this limit should be increased to, say, 14/16 and 15/16 of the maximum limit, and/or be disabled by default?  That is still 1.5M and 780k entries from the maximum, assuming 64-char names == 12M entries maximum.

It should also be noted that this message will only be printed the FIRST time the directory grows to this size, and not if the entries are deleted and recreated, since deleting entries does not shrink the directory (that would be a separate and complex patch).

Patch Set 9:

Hi Andreas,

I did a test which create 32, 64, 255 length files
(files like aaaaa1,aaaa2,aaaa3….)

when they hit enospc firstly, used space of directory is about 560M space.
(560M/750M)=0.74.

Yeah here filename is a little regular, but such behaviors
is what users tend to do, for example, file1,file2,file3...

I am wondering if it might be better we used
500M as second warning and 450M as first time warning.
and rate here 3/5 and 2/3 might be more correct.

Regards,
Shilong

Patch Set 9:

So this shows that the 3/4 of max theoretical size is a good limit for the actual ENOSPC. If the warning limits should be at 450MB and 500MB then that is 9/11 and 10/11 of 550MB. That works out to be 13/16 and 15/16 if we want to avoid using expensive division.

Patch Set 9:

Hi Andreas,

i don't understand  where 550M you mean come from.
3/4 of max limit(508 * 511 * 4096) should be 760M

So 450/760 500/760 should be 10/16, 11/16 here.

Am i missing something here?

Regards,
Shilong

parents.subject: LU-7543 obd: reserve connection flag OBD_CONNECT_FLAGS2

commit.subject: LU-6824 ldiskfs: give warning with dir htree growing
commit.message:
LU-6824 ldiskfs: give warning with dir htree growing

Currently without large dir feature, ldiskfs directory hash tree
will be limited 2 height, this means directory size is limited about
1GB, and in fact users are likely to hit ENOSPC when reaching half of
limit because of bad hash. tested by following scripts.

i=0
filename="aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa"
"aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaabbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbb"
while [ 1 ]
do
        touch $filename"$i"
        if [ $? -ne 0 ];then
                break
        fi
        ((i++))
done

When directoy size grow about 590M, we hit ENOSPC. Better way is to
add support to e2fsprogs so that we can use large dir feature.
As a walkaround way, this patch try to give warning messages to
console when 10/16 and 11/16 of limit reach.

So this patch will try to give following messages when warning
limit or hash index tree limit reach:

Directory (inode: 8388610 FID: [0x200000401:0x1:0x0]) has approached
maximum limit.

Directory (inode: 8388610 FID: [0x200000401:0x1:0x0]) is approaching
maximum limit.

FID output here is useful for administrators to locate lustre
file path

Signed-off-by: Wang Shilong <wshilong@ddn.com>
Change-Id: I7f78c421bbb89f76298e0174cc46d774ea82eb06
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/15548
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Yang Sheng <yang.sheng@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: James Simmons <uja.ornl@yahoo.com>
